  • Conclusion

Global Government: Will Democracy Prevail?

Author

Franchesca Garcia, Jett Palmer, Kaatje Matthews-vanKoetsveld

Context

This report lifts the curtain on some of the social and welfare markers that are associated with democratic and non-democratic regimes. When a country overthrows democracy, how long does the new regime last? What contributes to its downfall? We’ll start by exploring our three, key datasets for this report.

Democracy and Dictatorship

The Democracy and Dictatorship (DD) data were released as part of TidyTuesday on November 5, 2024. TidyTuesday is a weekly project that shares open datasets for users to practice data analysis. This version updates the dataset from Bjørnskov and Rode (2020), building on earlier work by Cheibub et al. (2010) and Alvarez et al. (1996). The dataset was later cleaned and shared on GitHub by Jon Harmon. Bjørnskov and Rode expanded existing data on political regimes by adding more countries, institutional detail, and a clear indicator for both successful and failed coups. They defined coups using three criteria: the goal must be to overthrow the executive, the actors must be connected to the state, and the event must last no longer than a week. This provides a consistent way to identify and compare regime changes. The data include the regime types of countries around the world between the years 1950 and 2020. Each observation (row) represents a country and year. The dataset focuses primarily on whether a country is democratic or not. Using this structure, we can study how often countries move away from democracy and how long those periods last which we call “spells.” Building on this, our analysis applies unique visualization techniques and survival analysis methods to examine the duration and hazard of non-democratic spells.

World Happiness Report

With the first World Happiness report released on April 1st of 2012 by the United Nations General Assembly, this report has now captured self-perception data on global happiness for a near-consecutive 13 years. Today, the survey is supported by the University of Oxford and Gallup and represents over 140 countries. The report was established by the United Nations with the purpose of “giv[ing] more importance to happiness and well-being in determining how to achieve and measure social and economic development” (World Happiness Report). Each year, more than 100,000 people take the survey and are asked a happiness ranking question that is translated into their native language.

Code 
# The following code webscrapes the data from the world happiness reports via the Wikipedia webpage
library(tidyverse)
library(rvest)

#Reads the html from the World Happiness Report Wikipedia page
WHR <- read_html("https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/World_Happiness_Report")

# There are 39 tables, so we need to reduce to the ones we want
# Get the header nodes
headers <- WHR |> 
  html_elements("h3")
header_text <- headers |> 
  html_text(trim = TRUE)

# Creates the patterns to match the header nodes
years <- 2013:2025
targets <- paste0(years, " report")

# Only keeps the report headers
target_idx <- str_detect(header_text, str_c(targets, collapse = "|"))
target_headers <- headers[target_idx]
target_labels <- header_text[target_idx]


# Function takes table following the specified header. 
get_WHR_table <- function(header) {
  header |>
    html_element(xpath = "following::table[1]") |>
    html_table(fill=TRUE)
}

# Retreive tables
WHR_tables <- target_headers |>
  map(get_WHR_table)

# Add year column
clean_whr <- function(df, year) {
  if (year == 2016) {
    # Third row is the real column names
    colnames(df) <- as.character(unlist(df[3, ]))
    # drop the 2 rows with messy column names
    df <- df[-c(1, 2, 3), 1:4]
  } else {
    # Second row is the real column names
    colnames(df) <- as.character(unlist(df[2, ]))
    # drop the 2 rows with messy column names
    df <- df[-c(1, 2), ]
    # drop extra empty columns
    df <- df[, !is.na(colnames(df))]
  }

  # reset row names
  rownames(df) <- NULL
  # Add year as a column
  df <- df |> 
    mutate(year = as.integer(year))
  
  # Year specific problems
  if (year == 2013) {
    df |> 
      rename(country_name = Country,
             `Overall rank` = `Rank[61]`,
             Score = Happiness)
  } else if (year == 2016) {
    df |>
      rename(country_name = Country,
             `Overall rank` = `Overall Rank[58][59]`)
  } else if (year ==  2017) {
    df |> 
      filter_out(Country %in% c("Europe[a]", "World")) |>
      rename(country_name = Country,
             `Overall rank` = `Overall Rank`)
  } else if (year == 2023) {
    df |> 
      mutate(`Country or region` = str_remove_all(`Country or region`, 
                                                   "\\*")) |> 
      rename(country_name = `Country or region`) |> 
      slice(-n())
  } else {
    df |> 
      rename(country_name = `Country or region`)
  }
}

# runs function on the list of dataframes to clean
# Different tables have different issues
years <- c(2025, 2024, 2023, 2022, 2020, 2019, 2018, 2017, 2016, 2013)
WHR_tables2 <- map2(WHR_tables, years, .f = clean_whr)
#Bind the rows of all the individual year tables
WHR_table <- bind_rows(WHR_tables2)

World Bank Group

World Bank Group is a collection of internationally-operating organizations that aim to reduce global poverty through sustainable development and financing. Their operations and mission are highly data-driven, and they provide a breadth of open-source, global welfare data to the public via their API. Most datasets date back several decades, represent hundreds of countries, and vary on completeness. Datasets associated with their products and services (e.g., lending portfolios, asset management) are created and updated internally. Other global development data are sourced via World Bank partners, including governments, international agencies, regional development banks, donors, and others. We accessed country-level data on population, life-expectancy, and education-related variables.

Code 
# The following code accesses country level data from the World Bank API
library(httr)
library(tidyjson)
library(jsonlite)
library(glue)

# Create function that accesses country-level variable from World Bank API

get_indicator <- function(country_code, indicator_code) {
  
  url <- glue("https://api.worldbank.org/v2/country/{country_code}/indicator/{indicator_code}?format=json&per_page=100")
  
  if(httr::status_code(GET(url)) == 200){
  
  json <- GET(url) |>
    (\(x) x$content)() |>
    rawToChar() |>
    fromJSON()
  
    if (length(json) < 2 || is.null(json[[2]])) {
      return(NULL)
    }
  
    if(is.data.frame(json[[2]])) {
      indicator_name = str_to_snake(json[[2]]$indicator$value[1])
      
      json[[2]] |>
      select(country_code = countryiso3code, 
             year = date, 
             value) |>
      mutate(year = as.integer(year),
             indicator = indicator_name)
    }
    else{
      return(NULL)
    }
  }
  else{
    return(NULL)
  }
}

# Create vectors for unique country codes and variables of interest based on World Bank variable codes

country_codes <- democracy_data$country_code |>
  unique()

indicators <- c("SP.POP.TOTL",
                "SP.POP.GROW",
                "SP.DYN.CBRT.IN",
                "SP.DYN.CDRT.IN",
                "SP.DYN.LE00.MA.IN",
                "SP.DYN.LE00.FE.IN",
                "SE.PRM.ENRR",
                "SE.SEC.ENRR",
                "SE.TER.ENRR")

# Iterate function over countries and variables of interest

people_data <- list()

for (c in country_codes) {
  for (i in indicators) {

    temp <- get_indicator(c, i)

    if (!is.null(temp)) {
      people_data[[length(people_data) + 1]] <- temp
    }
  }
}

people <- bind_rows(people_data)

people <- people |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = indicator,
              values_from = value)

We’ll explore the following research questions throughout this report.

Research Questions

  1. How long does it take for countries that transition away from democracy to return to it and what factors influence this?
  2. How do welfare indicators (e.g., population, happiness, life expectancy) differ across political regimes?
Code 
# packages here
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(countrycode)
library(sf)
library(rnaturalearth)
library(rnaturalearthdata)
library(ggtext)
library(here)
library(ggalluvial)
library(ggfittext)
library(paletteer)
library(survival)
library(gtsummary)
library(htmltools)
Code 
# Load in master dataset joined from 3 sources above
# We saved this locally to avoid re-scraping on every render
democracy <- read_csv(here("Data", "democracy.csv"))

# Save global map geometries
world <- ne_countries(scale = "medium", returnclass = "sf")

# Combine democracy data with global map geometries
world_democracy <- world |>
  left_join(democracy, by = c("adm0_a3" = "country_code"))

Exploring Non-Democratic Spells

Let’s start by taking a pulse on the modern-day presence and absence of democracy around the world. Figure 1 shows countries’ regime types in 2020, with democratic countries in blue and non-democratic countries in red. Non-democratic regimes are more concentrated in regions such as Africa, the Middle East, and Southeast Asia.

World Map of Democracy

Code 
world_democracy |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = factor(is_democracy,
                            levels = c(FALSE, TRUE),
                            labels = c("No", "Yes"))),
          linewidth = 0.05) +
  labs(title = "<span style='color:steelblue'><b>Democratic</b></span> and <span style='color:#ee6b6e'><b>Non-Democratic</b></span> Regimes Around the Globe",
       subtitle = "Non-Democratic Regimes Conentrated In Africa & Asia",
       alt = "World map showing countries colored by regime type. Democratic countries are shown in blue, and non-democratic countries are shown in red.") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("No" = "#ee6b6e",
                               "Yes" = "steelblue")) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        plot.title = element_markdown())

A Sankey plot can help us visualize regime-change patterns on the global scale. We see that most transitions into democratic regimes originate from military or civilian dictatorships. There’s also substantial movement between civilian dictatorships and military dictatorships.

Flow of Change in Regime Types

Code 
# create frequency data for sankey plot

regime_change_freq <- democracy |>
  arrange(country_code, year) |>
  group_by(country_code) |>
  mutate(post_regime = lead(regime_category)) |>
  filter((year == min(year) 
          & regime_category != post_regime) | 
           regime_category != post_regime)  |>
  mutate(regime_category = ifelse(regime_category %in% c("Parliamentary democracy",
                                                         "Mixed democratic",
                                                         "Presidential democracy",
                                                         "Civilian dictatorship",
                                                         "Military dictatorship",
                                                         "Royal dictatorship"),
                                  str_to_title(regime_category),
                                  "Other"),
         post_regime = ifelse(post_regime %in% c("Parliamentary democracy",
                                                 "Mixed democratic",
                                                 "Presidential democracy",
                                                 "Civilian dictatorship",
                                                 "Military dictatorship",
                                                 "Royal dictatorship"),
                              str_to_title(post_regime),
                              "Other")) |>
  ungroup(country_code) |>
  count(regime_category, post_regime)


# reorder regime categories from democratic to dictatorial

regime_change_freq <- regime_change_freq |>
  mutate(regime_category  = factor(regime_category,
                                   levels = c("Royal Dictatorship",
                                              "Military Dictatorship",
                                              "Civilian Dictatorship",
                                              "Presidential Democracy",
                                              "Mixed Democratic",
                                              "Parliamentary Democracy",
                                              "Other")),
         post_regime = factor(post_regime,
                              levels = c("Royal Dictatorship",
                                         "Military Dictatorship",
                                         "Civilian Dictatorship",
                                         "Presidential Democracy",
                                         "Mixed Democratic",
                                         "Parliamentary Democracy",
                                         "Other")))

# create sankey plot

cols <- c(
  "#E69F00",
  "#009E73",
  "#56B4E9",
  "#F0E442",
  "#0072B2",
  "#ee6b6e",
  "#808080"
)

regime_change_freq |>
  ggplot(aes(y = n,
             axis1 = regime_category,
             axis2 = post_regime)) +
  geom_alluvium(aes(fill = as.factor(regime_category))) +
  geom_stratum() +
  geom_fit_text(stat = "stratum",
                aes(label = str_wrap(after_stat(stratum), width = 12)),
                width = 0.25) +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(title = "Regime Changes",
       x = "Regime",
       y = "Frequency",
       fill = "Old Regime",
       alt = "Sankey plot showing changes in political regime categories from old to new. Flows are colored by the old, or itinital, regime of the change. Regime categories for both axes include Royal Dictatorship, Military Dictatorship, Civilian Dictatorship, Presidential Democracy, Mixed Democracy, and Parliamentary Democracy (from least to most democratic). Each axis also includes an Other category comprised of non sovereign nations. Most changes to democracies are from military or civilian dictatorships.") +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("Old Regime", "New Regime")) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = cols)

Cutting across decades of political history, we can identify the countries with the greatest proportion of dictator-led regimes and the greatest number of regime changes. Table 1 summarizes the number of regime types by country and the percentage classified as dictatorships over the 1950-2020 period, noting that data may be incomplete for some countries. Countries with a larger number of regimes show extreme variation in dictatorship prevalence, ranging from 0% to 100%.

Percent of Dictatorships Regimes by Country

Code 
# Calculates the number of regime changes for a given country
count_regimes <- function(country) {

  democracy |>
    filter(country_code == country) |>
    arrange(year) |>
    mutate(prev_regime = lag(regime_category)) |>
    filter(year == min(year) | regime_category != prev_regime) |>
    nrow()
  
}

# Calculates the number of regimes that were dictatorships for a given country
# Regime index of 3, 4, or 5 indicates a dictatorship
count_dictatorships <- function(country) {

  democracy |>
    filter(country_code == country) |>
    arrange(year) |>
    mutate(prev_regime = lag(regime_category)) |>
    filter(year == min(year) | regime_category != prev_regime, 
           regime_category_index >= 3) |>
    nrow()
  
}


table1 <- democracy |>
  distinct(country_code, country_name) |>
  # Uses map to iteratively apply the custom functions above and calculates the 
  # proportion of regimes that were/are dictatorships in each country
  mutate(n_regimes = map_dbl(country_code, count_regimes),
         n_dicts = map_dbl(country_code, count_dictatorships),
         prop_dict = n_dicts/n_regimes) |>
  select(country_name, n_regimes, prop_dict) |>
  arrange(desc(n_regimes)) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(country_name = "Country", 
             n_regimes = "Number of Regimes",
             prop_dict = "Percent Dictatorship") |>
  tab_style(cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_column_labels(everything())) |>
  fmt_percent(columns = prop_dict) |>
  data_color(prop_dict,
             palette = "Blues") |>
  tab_caption(caption = md("Table 1. Counts of regimes per country, with the percent of regimes that were dictatorships"))

div(
  style = "max-height: 400px; overflow-y: auto;",
  table1
)

Table 2 summarizes the percentage of country-year observations classified under each form of governance. Note that these categories are not mutually exclusive, as a country can fall into multiple forms at once (e.g., a country can be both democratic and presidential). Democracy is most prevalent in North and Latin America. Communism constitutes over 32% of European and Central Asian regimes.

Governance Types by World Region

Code 
democracy <- democracy |>
  # Creates the region column using the countrycode package
  mutate(region = countrycode(country_code,
                              origin = 'iso3c',
                              destination = 'region',
                              # Custom matches for the codes that don't find match
                              custom_match = c('ZAR' = 'Sub-Saharan Africa',
                                               'GER' = 'Europe & Central Asia',
                                               'NUR' = 'East Asia & Pacific',
                                               'PRI' = 'Latin America & Caribbean',
                                               'ROM' = 'Europe & Central Asia'
                                               )
                              )
         )

democracy |>
  group_by(region) |>
  # Proportions of each form of governance
  summarize(n = n(),
            prop_demo = sum(is_democracy, na.rm = TRUE)/n,
            prop_pres = sum(is_presidential, na.rm = TRUE)/n,
            prop_mon = sum(is_monarchy, na.rm = TRUE)/n,
            prop_comw = sum(is_commonwealth, na.rm = TRUE)/n,
            prop_colon = sum(is_colony, na.rm = TRUE)/n,
            prop_comm = sum(is_communist, na.rm = TRUE)/n
            ) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(region = "World Region", 
             n = "Number of Observations",
             prop_demo = "Percent Democratic",
             prop_pres = "Percent Presidential",
             prop_mon = "Percent Monarchy",
             prop_comw = "Percent Commonwealth",
             prop_colon = "Percent Colony",
             prop_comm = "Percent Communist",
             ) |>
  tab_style(cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_column_labels(everything())) |>
  fmt_percent(columns = -c(n, region)) |>
  fmt_integer(columns = n) |> 
  tab_caption(caption = md("Table 2. Percent of observations under each form of governance, by World Region")) |>
  tab_spanner(label = "Governance Type (%)",
              columns = -c(n, region)) |>
  data_color(columns = -c(n, region),
             palette = "Blues")
Table 2. Percent of observations under each form of governance, by World Region

Governance Type (%)
Percent Democratic Percent Presidential Percent Monarchy Percent Commonwealth Percent Colony Percent Communist

Sub-Saharan Africa 3,408 18.37% 67.78% 6.31% 26.91% 19.42% 5.96%

A statistical model for the time until a country that lost democracy returns to democracy (i.e., a democratic spell) can help us investigate these regional and regime characteristics more formally.

Modeling

Code 
# Survival model setup

survival_base <- democracy |>
  arrange(country_code, year) |>
  group_by(country_code) |>
  mutate(lost_democracy = is_democracy == FALSE & lag(is_democracy, default = TRUE),
         spell_id = cumsum(lost_democracy),
         next_is_democracy = lead(is_democracy))

spell_starts <- survival_base |>
  filter(lost_democracy == TRUE) |>
  select(country_code,
         spell_id,
         region,
         is_communist,
         is_multiparty)

survival <- survival_base |>
  filter(is_democracy == FALSE) |>
  group_by(country_code, spell_id) |>
  summarize(start_year = min(year),
            end_year = max(year),
            returned_to_democracy = any(next_is_democracy == TRUE, na.rm = TRUE),
            time = end_year - start_year + 1,
            .groups = "drop")

survival <- survival |>
  left_join(spell_starts, by = c("country_code", "spell_id"))

# Survival analysis model
km <- survfit(Surv(time, returned_to_democracy) ~ 1,
              data = survival)

plot(km,
     xlab = "Years Since Losing Democracy",
     ylab = "")

title(main = "Probability of Remaing Non-Democratic Decreases Over Time", adj = 0)
Code 
# Cox Proportional Hazards Model
cox_model_new <- coxph(Surv(time, returned_to_democracy) ~
                         is_communist +
                         is_multiparty +
                         strata(region) +
                         cluster(country_code),
                       data = survival)

# ph_test <- cox.zph(cox_model_new)
# ph_test

# summary(cox_model_new)

tbl_regression(cox_model_new,
               exponentiate = TRUE,
               label = list(
                 is_communist ~ "Communist",
                 is_multiparty ~ "Multiparty System"
                 )
               )|> 
  bold_labels() |>
  as_gt() |>
  tab_style(cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_column_labels(everything())) |>
  tab_caption(caption = md("Cox Proportional Hazards Model Coefficent Estimates"))
Cox Proportional Hazards Model Coefficent Estimates
Characteristic HR 95% CI p-value
Communist


    FALSE — —
    TRUE 0.04 0.01, 0.25 <0.001
Multiparty System


    FALSE — —
    TRUE 0.93 0.67, 1.29 0.7
Abbreviations: CI = Confidence Interval, HR = Hazard Ratio

There are 255 non-democratic spells, 178 of which return to democracy. The Cox Proportional Hazards Model significantly predicts “time to democracy” (p = 0.003).

Hazard Ratio Interpretations:

  • Communist regimes are about 96% less likely to return to democracy compared to non-communist regimes (p < 0.001).

  • There is insufficient evidence to conclude that multiparty systems affect the likelihood of a non-democratic regime returning to democracy (p = 0.65).

Note: The number of predictors in this model were reduced to satisfy proportional hazard and independence assumptions.

Relationships with Global Welfare

Again, let’s start by taking a pulse on the status of global population. Figure 3 shows the distribution of population across countries, with darker shades of blue indicating larger populations. Population is heavily concentrated in a few countries, notably China and India, while much of Africa, South America, and Oceania has relatively lower population densities by comparison.

World Map of Population

Code 
# happiness or population

world_democracy |>
  ggplot() +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = population_total),
          linewidth = 0.05) +
  labs(
    title = "<b>Global Population Concentrated In Asia</b>",
    fill = "Population",
    alt = "World map showing countries shaded by total population, using a blue color gradient.") +
  scale_fill_gradient(
    low = "#deebf7",
    high = "#08519c",
    na.value = "grey90") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    legend.position = "right",
    plot.title = element_markdown())

Another country-level characteristic that we think may be related with political regimes is life expectancy. Figure 4 shows that life expectancy generally increases over time across all regime types. More democratic regimes tend to have higher average life expectancy compared to less democratic ones across all time.

Life Expectancy by Regime Type

Code 
# Create set of "default regimes" which consist of the top five most common

default_regimes <- c(
  "Parliamentary democracy",
  "Presidential democracy",
  "Mixed democratic",
  "Civilian dictatorship",
  "Military dictatorship"
)

# Create graph of life expectancy over time by regime category (top 5)

# This data wrangling step allows for the color gradient to match the
# life expectancy order and computes the averages

plot_data <- democracy |>
  filter(regime_category %in% default_regimes) |>
  mutate(
    avg_life_expectancy = (
      life_expectancy_at_birth_male_years +
      life_expectancy_at_birth_female_years) / 2) |>
  filter(
    !is.na(year),
    !is.na(avg_life_expectancy)) |>
  group_by(year, regime_category) |>
  summarize(
    n = n(),
    avg_life_expectancy = mean(avg_life_expectancy, na.rm = TRUE))

regime_order <- plot_data |>
  filter(year == 2020) |>
  arrange(avg_life_expectancy) |>
  pull(regime_category)

plot_data |>
  mutate(
    regime_category = factor(regime_category, levels = regime_order)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = year, 
             y = avg_life_expectancy, 
             color = regime_category)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c(
      "#c6dbef",
      "#9ecae1",
      "#6baed6",
      "#3182bd",
      "#08519c")) +
  labs(
    title = "<b>Average Life Expectancy Increases Over Time Across Regimes</b>",
    x = "Year",
    y = NULL,
    color = "Regime Category",
    alt = "Line plot of average life expectancy over time in years, colored by regime type. Regime Types include: Parliamentary democracy, mixed democratic, presidential democracy, civilian dictatorship, and military dictaorship. All 5 lines tend to increase over time. The more democratic regimes tend to have higher lines, indicating a higher average life expectancy.") +
  theme_minimal() +
  guides(color = guide_legend(reverse = TRUE)) +
  theme(plot.title = element_markdown())

The four most common political regimes around the world in no particular order are:

  • Civilian dictatorships

  • Parliamentary democracies

  • Military dictatorships

  • Presidential democracies

Let’s compare these regimes by their implementation of democratic principles, like suffrage and fair elections. Table 3 shows the percentage of country-year observations that exhibit five different democratic principles. Parliamentary and presidential democracies have high percentages across all principles. In contrast, civilian and military dictatorships rarely meet this criteria, with the exception of full voting rights, which appears relatively more common.

Democratic Principles for Top Regimes

Code 
# regimes is optional input as a vector of regime categories
# the default argument looks at the 4 we wish to compare
summarize_regimes <- function(regimes = c("Civilian dictatorship",
                                          "Parliamentary democracy",
                                          "Military dictatorship",
                                          "Presidential democracy")) {
  
  # For each regime type the proportion of observations with each democratic
  # principle is calculated
  democracy |>
    filter(regime_category %in% regimes) |>
    group_by(regime_category) |>
    summarize(
      n = n(),
      prop_demo = sum(is_democracy, na.rm = TRUE) / n,
      prop_suffrage = sum(has_full_suffrage, na.rm = TRUE) / n,
      prop_multiparty = sum(is_multiparty, na.rm = TRUE) / n,
      prop_free_fair = sum(has_free_and_fair_election, na.rm = TRUE) / n,
      prop_alternation = sum(has_alternation, na.rm = TRUE) / n
    ) |>
    gt() |>
    cols_label(
      regime_category = "Regime",
      n = "Number of Observations",
      prop_demo = "Democratic",
      prop_suffrage = "Full Voting Rights",
      prop_multiparty = "Multi-Party Elections",
      prop_free_fair = "Free & Fair Elections",
      prop_alternation = "Rotating Top Office"
    ) |>
    tab_style(
      cell_text(weight = "bold"),
      locations = cells_column_labels(everything())
    ) |>
    fmt_percent(columns = -c(n, regime_category)) |>
    fmt_integer(columns = n) |> 
    tab_caption(
      caption = md("Table 3. Percent of observations demonstrating democratic principles, by regime category")
    ) |>
    tab_spanner(
      label = "Democratic Principles Represented (% Observations)",
      columns = -c(n, regime_category)
    ) |>
    data_color(
      columns = -c(n, regime_category),
      palette = "Blues"
    )
}

summarize_regimes()
Table 3. Percent of observations demonstrating democratic principles, by regime category
Regime Number of Observations
Democratic Principles Represented (% Observations)
Democratic Full Voting Rights Multi-Party Elections Free & Fair Elections Rotating Top Office
Civilian dictatorship 2,998 0.03% 93.70% 52.43% 3.14% 0.47%
Military dictatorship 1,892 0.05% 90.91% 40.12% 0.26% 0.05%
Parliamentary democracy 2,660 99.89% 97.26% 99.89% 99.89% 99.89%
Presidential democracy 1,509 99.87% 92.05% 99.87% 99.87% 99.67%

We’re also interested in comparing citizens’ perceived happiness and life satisfaction around the world. Table 4 shows that military and civilian dictatorships tend to have lower reported happiness, whereas parliamentary democracies and royal dictatorships tend to have higher reported happiness.

Happiness by Regime Type

Code 
# Table numeric summary of happiness score by regime.
happiness_dist <- function(regime) {
  democracy |>
    filter(regime_category == regime) |>
    filter_out(is.na(score)) |>
    summarize(n=n(),
              avg_happy = mean(score),
              sd_happy = sd(score),
              min_happy = min(score),
              med_happy = median(score),
              max_happy = max(score)
    )
}

democracy |>
  distinct(regime_category) |>
  mutate(map_df(.x = regime_category,
                .f = happiness_dist)) |>
  filter(n >= 10) |> 
  arrange(desc(avg_happy)) |>
  gt() |>
  cols_label(regime_category = "Regime", 
             n = "Number of Observations",
             avg_happy = "Average Happiness Score",
             sd_happy = "Standard Deviation Happiness Score",
             min_happy = "Minimum Happiness Score",
             med_happy = "Median Happiness Score",
             max_happy = "Maximum Happiness Score"
             ) |>
  tab_style(cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_column_labels(everything())) |>
  fmt_number(columns = -c(n, regime_category), decimals = 2) |> 
  tab_caption(caption = md("Table 4. Happiness Score Summary by Regime Category")) |>
  tab_footnote(footnote = "Happiness Score scraped from World Happiness Report 
  Wikipedia page, Scores are out of 10") |>
  data_color(columns = -c(n, regime_category),
             palette = "Blues")
Table 4. Happiness Score Summary by Regime Category
Regime Number of Observations Average Happiness Score Standard Deviation Happiness Score Minimum Happiness Score Median Happiness Score Maximum Happiness Score
Parliamentary democracy 181 6.26 0.98 3.57 6.31 7.69
Royal dictatorship 41 6.05 0.63 4.63 6.23 7.14
Presidential democracy 177 5.68 1.02 3.41 5.80 7.65
Mixed democratic 130 5.61 1.10 3.48 5.44 7.81
Civilian dictatorship 210 4.92 0.82 2.57 5.00 7.34
Military dictatorship 58 3.96 0.85 2.69 3.71 6.47
Happiness Score scraped from World Happiness Report Wikipedia page, Scores are out of 10

Conclusion

This report examined several significant relationships among political regime, geographic, and welfare indicators. Although democracies are sometimes overthrown, they are often restored by the people they serve. The analysis of non-democratic spells suggest that region, civil liberties, and political priorities play important roles in the sustainability of a governmental system.
